title: PF Ratio Calculator - Protein Fraction Ratio Analysis
tagline: Calculate protein fraction ratios from blood test results to assess protein metabolism, liver function, and nutritional status.
intro: Enter your serum protein electrophoresis values to compute albumin/globulin ratio, fraction percentages, and clinical pattern interpretation.

error.invalid: Please enter valid numbers for all required protein fraction fields.
error.albumin: Albumin value must be greater than zero.
error.globulin: Total globulin must be greater than zero.

result.heading: Protein Fraction Results
result.low_ag: Low A/G ratio — possible liver disease, chronic inflammation, or immune disorder
result.high_ag: High A/G ratio — possible hypogammaglobulinemia or hypoalbuminemia
result.elevated_gamma: Elevated gamma globulins — chronic inflammation or immune activation

about.heading: About the PF Ratio Calculator
about.body:
The PF Ratio Calculator is a clinical tool that analyzes the relationship between different protein fractions in blood serum to evaluate protein metabolism, liver function, nutritional status, and various medical conditions. Unlike a simple albumin/globulin ratio, this calculator breaks down the full protein electrophoresis panel into albumin, alpha-1, alpha-2, beta, and gamma globulin fractions, providing a comprehensive picture of the body's protein balance.

Blood serum proteins are separated into distinct fractions by serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP), a laboratory technique that uses an electrical field to migrate proteins across a gel or capillary based on their charge and size. Each fraction represents a group of proteins with similar physical properties and distinct biological functions. Albumin, which normally constitutes 55–65% of total serum protein, is produced exclusively in the liver and serves as the primary determinant of oncotic pressure, the osmotic force that keeps fluid within blood vessels. It also acts as a carrier protein, transporting hormones, fatty acids, bilirubin, calcium, and many drugs throughout the bloodstream.

The globulin fractions are divided into four subgroups. Alpha-1 globulins (0.1–0.4 g/dL in healthy adults) include alpha-1 antitrypsin, alpha-1 acid glycoprotein, and other acute phase reactants that rise rapidly in response to inflammation or tissue damage. Alpha-2 globulins (0.4–1.0 g/dL) include haptoglobin, ceruloplasmin, and alpha-2 macroglobulin, all of which also participate in the acute phase response and are elevated during inflammatory states. Beta globulins (0.6–1.2 g/dL) include transferrin, complement proteins, and beta-lipoproteins. Gamma globulins (0.7–1.6 g/dL) are primarily immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM, IgD, IgE) produced by plasma cells and represent the humoral immune response.

The albumin/globulin (A/G) ratio — calculated by dividing albumin by the sum of all globulin fractions — is a sensitive marker for a wide range of conditions. A normal A/G ratio falls between 1.1 and 2.5. A low A/G ratio, particularly below 1.0, is clinically significant and may reflect decreased albumin synthesis (as in cirrhosis, hepatitis, or severe malnutrition), increased globulin production (as in chronic infection, autoimmune disease, or multiple myeloma), or protein loss (as in nephrotic syndrome). A high A/G ratio above 2.5 may indicate hypogammaglobulinemia.

Clinical pattern recognition is central to interpreting protein fraction results. In acute inflammation, alpha-1 and alpha-2 fractions rise while albumin may fall slightly. In chronic liver disease, albumin decreases progressively as hepatic synthetic function deteriorates, while gamma globulins often rise due to increased immunoglobulin production by liver-associated lymphoid tissue. In nephrotic syndrome, albumin, alpha-1, and gamma globulins fall due to renal loss, while alpha-2 globulins rise compensatorily. In multiple myeloma or other plasma cell dyscrasias, a monoclonal spike (M-spike) appears in the beta or gamma region.

This calculator does not replace formal SPEP interpretation by a clinical laboratory, but it is an excellent educational and screening tool for healthcare students, professionals, and patients who want to understand their laboratory results. Always interpret protein fraction data in the context of the patient's clinical presentation, medical history, other laboratory values, and any current medications or treatments that might affect protein levels.

examples.heading: PF Ratio Calculation Examples
examples.intro: Common protein fraction patterns and their clinical significance across different health states.

items.note: Balanced protein fractions consistent with a healthy adult. Total globulin 3.1 g/dL, albumin 57.5% of total.
Acute inflammatory pattern: elevated alpha-1 and alpha-2, reduced albumin. Consistent with active infection or inflammatory disease.
Liver disease pattern: markedly decreased albumin, elevated gamma globulins. Suggestive of chronic hepatitis or cirrhosis.
Immune disorder pattern: normal albumin but markedly elevated gamma globulins. Possible autoimmune condition or chronic infection.

howto.heading: How to Use the PF Ratio Calculator
steps: Obtain a recent serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) report from a certified laboratory. Ensure all values are in g/dL.
Enter your albumin value (normal range: 3.5–5.0 g/dL) and each of the four globulin fractions (alpha-1, alpha-2, beta, gamma) in the respective input fields.
Optionally enter the total protein value. This allows the calculator to verify accuracy and display each fraction as a percentage of total protein.
Click 'Calculate PF Ratio' to see the albumin/globulin ratio, total globulin, fraction percentages, and clinical pattern interpretation.
Compare your results against the normal reference ranges displayed. Consult a healthcare professional for clinical interpretation, especially if values fall outside normal limits.

items.q: What is the normal albumin/globulin (A/G) ratio?
items.a: The normal A/G ratio is typically 1.1 to 2.5. A ratio above 1.0 is generally considered normal, indicating that albumin slightly exceeds the total globulin fraction. Ratios below 1.0 are clinically significant and warrant further investigation for liver disease, inflammatory conditions, or immune disorders.

items.q: What does a low A/G ratio indicate?
items.a: A low A/G ratio can indicate decreased albumin production (liver disease, malnutrition), increased globulin production (chronic infections, autoimmune diseases, multiple myeloma), or albumin loss (nephrotic syndrome). The specific pattern of which fractions are abnormal helps narrow the differential diagnosis.

items.q: What are normal reference ranges for each protein fraction?
items.a: Normal adult ranges are: albumin 3.5–5.0 g/dL, alpha-1 globulin 0.1–0.4 g/dL, alpha-2 globulin 0.4–1.0 g/dL, beta globulin 0.6–1.2 g/dL, and gamma globulin 0.7–1.6 g/dL. Reference ranges may vary slightly between laboratories, so always compare against your laboratory's specific reference values.

items.q: How does liver disease affect protein fractions?
items.a: Liver disease progressively decreases albumin synthesis, leading to hypoalbuminemia. As the liver's synthetic capacity deteriorates, the A/G ratio falls. Chronic liver disease also typically elevates gamma globulins due to increased immunoglobulin production, further lowering the A/G ratio. Severe cirrhosis often shows albumin below 2.5 g/dL with markedly elevated gamma globulins.

items.q: What is the difference between albumin and total protein?
items.a: Total protein is the combined measurement of albumin and all globulin fractions. A normal total protein of 6.0–8.3 g/dL can mask significant abnormalities — for example, low albumin paired with high globulins may yield a normal total protein. The PF ratio breakdown reveals the distribution that total protein measurement alone cannot show.

items.q: Can medications affect protein fraction results?
items.a: Yes, several medications can affect protein levels. Corticosteroids and anabolic steroids may increase albumin. Chemotherapy agents, immunosuppressants, and certain antibiotics can decrease gamma globulins. Estrogen therapy can elevate alpha-2 globulins. Always inform your healthcare provider of all medications when interpreting protein electrophoresis results.
